options.text: Don't use bullet points or disclaimers
Please write this without using bullet points, markdown formatting, or disclaimers. I want natural writing.

Write in flowing, natural prose that gets straight to the content. Use a confident, conversational tone as if you're speaking directly to the reader. No bullet points, formatting, or introductory phrases like 'here's what you need to know.'

responses.response_weak: Sure, I can do that. Could you let me know what topic you'd like me to write about? I'll avoid bullet points and disclaimers. Just give me the subject and any other details, and I'll get started.
responses.response_medium:
Starting a business is one of those things that sounds simple until you actually try it. You have an idea, maybe even a good one, and then reality sets in. There are permits, taxes, branding decisions, and about a hundred small choices that nobody warns you about.

The good news is that none of it is as complicated as it feels in the beginning. Most of the overwhelm comes from trying to figure everything out at once instead of taking it one step at a time. The people who succeed aren't the ones who had it all figured out on day one. They're the ones who started before they felt ready and figured it out as they went.
responses.response_strong:
## A Response Written in Flowing, Confident Prose

There is a particular kind of courage that has nothing to do with grand gestures or dramatic leaps of faith. It lives in the quiet moment when you close your laptop at 11 PM, knowing the business plan still isn't perfect, and decide to launch anyway. It shows up when you price your work at what it's actually worth instead of what feels safe. It's there when you tell someone what you do for a living and, for the first time, don't add a disclaimer.

Building something from nothing is less about talent than it is about tolerance — tolerance for uncertainty, for looking foolish, for the long stretch of time between planting a seed and seeing anything grow. The founders who make it rarely describe some lightning-bolt moment of confidence. More often, they talk about stubbornness. About showing up on the days when showing up felt pointless. About trusting the process even when the process felt like it was broken.

The secret nobody tells you is that readiness is a myth. You will never feel ready. The gap between where you are and where you want to be will always look impossibly wide from the starting line. But the thing about gaps is that they only shrink from one direction — and it isn't the standing-still direction.

feedback.what_happened: The weak prompt says 'Don't use bullet points or disclaimers' — you'd mean 'write something that sounds natural and confident,' but the AI only hears what to avoid, not what to do, so it stalls and asks for clarification because it has no topic, no direction, and no idea what style you actually want. The medium prompt adds 'natural writing,' which gives the AI enough to produce decent prose, but the style is safe and generic because 'natural' could mean almost anything. The strong prompt flips every negative into a positive — flowing prose, confident tone, conversational, straight to the content — so the AI delivers writing with real voice, rhythm, and personality.
feedback.clarification_response: Got it — no bullet points or disclaimers! Could you tell me a bit more about what you'd like instead? For example: What topic should I write about? And what style are you going for — conversational and casual, polished and professional, or something else? Knowing what you want (not just what you don't) will help me get the tone right.
feedback.principle: Negative instructions ('don't do X') tell the AI what to avoid but leave infinite possibilities for what to do instead. Positive instructions ('write in flowing prose with a confident tone') give the AI a clear target.
feedback.tip: Flip every negative instruction into a positive one. Instead of 'don't use bullet points,' say 'write in flowing paragraphs.' Instead of 'don't be formal,' say 'use a warm, conversational tone.' Give the AI something to aim for, not just something to dodge.
